fields.title: Ray's Dry Cleaners
fields.body:
Ray Kowalski in the back of a dry cleaners he definitely doesn't own.
He slides a napkin across the table. The name is Marty. Get $800, keep 20%.
He adds "don't make a mess" in a way that suggests it hasn't worked before.
A second napkin sits on the table as you leave. Ray doesn't mention it.
Same street as Marty, different unit.

fields.title: The Walkup
fields.body: The building super is mopping the lobby. He's seen people like you before.

fields.title: Marty
fields.body:
Third floor. Elevator broken. Marty answers in a bathrobe at 2pm. Kind face.
Kid's drawings on the fridge. He had the money three weeks ago but his
daughter's car broke down. He has $200 now. He'll have the rest in two weeks.
His phone rings. He silences it. "That's my daughter. I'll call her back."

fields.title: The Muscle
fields.body: Marty goes to the bedroom. Player waits with the kid drawings.

fields.title: back home
fields.body: Home. Relative term. The radiator clanks. The window looks out on a brick wall. There's a corkboard on the back of the door where you used to pin things worth remembering. You've started using it for job leads instead. Old habits die. New ones move in.

fields.title: The Call
fields.body:
Three years inside and the city didn't wait for you. The apartment
  is exactly how you left it — which means it's a mess. Water stain
  on the ceiling shaped like a boot. Radiator that clanks like it's
  got opinions. The envelope of cash you came home with is already
  half gone and your parole officer wants you in legitimate work by
  the end of the month.

  Your old crew hasn't called.

  Then your phone buzzes. Your sister's name on the screen. You
  haven't spoken since the trial. Three years of silence and she's
  calling at nine on a Tuesday night. You stare at it for two rings.

fields.title: Her Voice
fields.body:
She doesn't say hello. Just launches into it like she's been
  rehearsing. There's a guy — Terrell — been coming around her
  building. Says she owes him for something her neighbor ran up.
  Wrong place, wrong association. Doesn't matter how it started.
  What matters is he's been at her door twice this week and the
  second time he put his hand on the frame and leaned in.

  You ask if she's okay. Long pause.

  "I'm fine. I just need it to stop."

  Another pause. Longer.

  "I didn't want to call you. I want you to know that."

fields.title: Why You
fields.body:
She lets the silence sit for a moment, like she's deciding how
  honest to be.

  "Because there's nobody else. Because you're already the worst
  thing that could happen to someone, so what's one more time."

  It lands like she meant it to. You don't argue. There's nothing
  to argue with.

fields.title: Squeeze
fields.body:
You tell him who you're there for. Then you tell him — quietly,
  without any particular emotion — what happens if he comes back.
  You don't raise your voice. You don't need to. The trick is
  making sure he understands that you mean it more than he does.

fields.title: The Fight
fields.body:
It goes loud and fast. Terrell's got reach but no discipline.
  The stoop light flickers. Somewhere above you a window slides shut.

fields.title: Backed Off
fields.body:
Terrell gets the message. However it was delivered.

  You go through his pockets before he has a chance to reconsider.
  A hundred and fifty in crumpled bills and a pair of brass knuckles
  with somebody else's initials scratched into the base. You pocket
  both. He doesn't object.

  You text your sister two words: "It's done."

  She doesn't reply. But she reads it. You see the tick.

fields.title: Done
fields.body:
Back in the apartment. Radiator clanking. The city outside doing
  what it always does. You set the brass knuckles on the counter
  and count the cash. A hundred and fifty. Not bad for a Tuesday.

  Your sister still hasn't texted back. But she will. Eventually.
  People always come back when they need something. The trick is
  being someone worth coming back to.

  The corkboard on the back of the door has one job pinned to it.
  Could be worse starts.

fields.title: Beaten
fields.body:
You come to on the pavement outside your sister's building. Ribs
  aching, wallet lighter. Someone took the liberty.

  You don't go up. You can't face that conversation right now.
  You just walk. The city swallows you up the way it always does —
  indifferent, unhurried, completely unimpressed.

fields.title: Terrell
fields.body:
Your sister's building is a six-story walkup off Decker Avenue.
  Terrell isn't hard to find — he's exactly where she said he'd be,
  leaning against the stoop railing like he owns the block. Young.
  Maybe twenty-five. Gold chain, clean sneakers, eyes that clock you
  the second you round the corner.

  He watches you approach without moving. "You lost?"

fields.title: The Corner
fields.body: You're on your corner. A payphone that never rings. A bodega with bars on the windows. The city moves around you like you're not there. For now, that suits you fine.

fields.title: Loading Dock
fields.body: The warehouse sits dark at the end of Pier 9. Two of Deluca's guys lean against the loading dock, smoking. They're not expecting trouble. They're also not moving.

fields.title: The Corner
fields.body: You find him on his usual corner, back to the wall, watching the street. He clocks you and his jaw tightens. You can step to him or let it go.

fields.title: The Case
fields.body: Russ finds you outside Sal's. He's holding a slim aluminum case and an unlit cigarette. Ten percent to walk something to a laundromat on Fifth. Hand it to a man in a gray jacket. Don't look inside.

fields.title: Crescent Street
fields.body: You find the captain finishing his rounds at the bodega on Crescent. Two runners flank him, but he sends them off when he clocks you coming. He's not worried. He should think harder about that.

fields.title: Dennis
fields.body:
Dennis is outside a laundromat he definitely doesn't own. He's wearing a suit
that's one size too small and sweating despite it being November. He explains
the job in the vaguest possible terms. The package sits between you on a
plastic chair. It makes no sound. It does nothing. It is simply there.

fields.title: The Board
fields.body: A corkboard inside Sal's back room. Folded notes, burner numbers, job descriptions written in the kind of language that doesn't hold up in court. Someone's moving product. Someone needs muscle. Someone wants a problem to disappear.

fields.title: Dock Fight
fields.body: The first guy reaches for something in his jacket. You don't give him the chance.

fields.title: Squaring Up
fields.body: He comes off the wall fast. Hands up, eyes wild. This is happening.

fields.title: Name Your Price
fields.body: Russ doesn't like it. He chews the unlit cigarette and does the math behind his eyes. You wait. He either agrees or you walk, and you both know it.

fields.title: Squaring Off
fields.body: He moves first — a short jab that says he does this for a living. You settle in.

fields.title: The Package
fields.body:
You are alone with it on the bus. It's heavier than it looks. Taped perfectly.
No label. Slightly warm. The city moves past the windows. You have a moment.

fields.title: Sal's Bar
fields.body: The kind of bar that doesn't have a sign. Sal keeps the lights low, the bottles cheap, and his mouth shut. Half the city's criminal ecosystem runs through this place on any given night.

fields.title: Side Entrance
fields.body: The guards don't even count the money before stepping aside. One of them points you toward a service entrance on the far side of the dock. 'Stay out of the back office,' he says. You note that.

fields.title: The Real Problem
fields.body: The corner boy's down, but a door opens across the street. Big guy, slow walk. He's been watching. Now it's his turn.

fields.title: Third Street Bridge
fields.body: Halfway across the bridge, you pick up a tail. Dark coat, measured pace. Could be nothing. The laundromat is two blocks further.

fields.title: Reminder
fields.body: He's better than you today. You end up against the bodega's shuttered front while he steps over you and tells his runners to move it. He doesn't even take your wallet. Worse — he doesn't need to.

fields.title: The Drop
fields.body:
An apartment building. Third floor. Unit 307. Knock twice.
Gloria answers immediately, like she was waiting behind the door. She takes
the package without looking at it. She looks at you instead, for slightly too
long. She hands you an envelope and closes the door.

fields.title: Inside the Warehouse
fields.body: Floor-to-ceiling shelving. The place is a maze. At the far end, two paths: one leads toward voices and the glow of a work lamp — someone's still here. The other leads toward rows of padlocked crates.

fields.title: Paid in Full
fields.body: The envelope is in your pocket. They'll think twice before shorting you again. You walk back to the square.

fields.title: Clean Exchange
fields.body: Eleven seconds. Gray jacket takes the case, hands you an envelope, walks. You don't watch him go. You count the bills two blocks later. Everything's there. Russ's cut is light. Yours isn't.

fields.title: Back to Normal
fields.body:
You are back where you started. Dennis is not there. The laundromat is empty.
His coffee cup is still on the windowsill. Still warm. No further explanation.

fields.title: Taken
fields.body: They're better than they looked. You wake up zip-tied to a chair in the back office. By the time someone cuts you loose, the warehouse is empty.

fields.title: Left on the Pavement
fields.body: You come to against a dumpster. Wallet's gone. Ribs ache. You limp back to the square with nothing.

fields.title: Made
fields.body: The tail is a vice detective with a long memory. You ditch the case in a trash can and spend an hour proving you were just walking. By the time you're clear, the buyer is gone. So is your cut.

fields.title: The Talk
fields.body: You lay it out. His crew is running tax on the wrong side of Crescent, and that ends tonight. He listens. Whether he thinks you're the kind of person who can back that up is another question.

fields.title: Clean Exit
fields.body: Nothing incriminating. Nothing worth the risk. You document what you see and walk out the way you came. Sometimes the job is just reconnaissance.

fields.title: Not Today
fields.body: You hold his gaze for a moment, then turn and walk. Some debts aren't worth the trouble. Today.

fields.title: Eighteen Percent
fields.body: Russ paid eighteen percent without much argument. The walk was clean, the handoff was fast, and you're back at the corner in under two hours. Some jobs go the way they're supposed to.

fields.title: No Blood
fields.body: He measures you for a long moment, then tells his runners to move it. They go. No blood, no debt — just a line redrawn without anyone having to fall. The bodega owner nods at you through the window.

fields.title: The Accountant
fields.body: The man with the ledgers turns out to be Deluca's bookkeeper. He's skimming. You now know things about Deluca's operation that Deluca doesn't know you know. That's worth more than anything in those crates.

fields.title: The Score
fields.body: Pharmacy-grade opioids. Twelve crates, all tagged for a hospital system that'll never see them. You make two calls. By morning, the crates are gone and you're owed a favor by the people who moved them.

fields.title: No Badge Saves You Here
fields.body: He's face-down on asphalt. You strip his wallet of the bribe money and disappear before anyone notices. Word will get around.

fields.title: The Second Call
fields.body:
Three days since Terrell. You'd almost let yourself think it was
over.

Your sister's name on the screen again. You pick up before the
second ring. She doesn't bother with the anger this time. Just
tells you there's been a car outside her building since yesterday
morning. Different plates each time, same two guys. She noticed
because she's been noticing everything since you left.

She doesn't ask if you can help. She just stays on the line.

fields.title: Casing It
fields.body:
You've been watching Marello's Fine Jewelers for a week. Long
enough to know the last staff member locks up at six-fifteen,
walks to the corner, catches the 47 bus. Long enough to know
the owner leaves separately, twenty minutes later, always the
same route. Long enough to have clocked the lockbox through the
window during a recon visit — you went in as a customer, asked
about a watch, left with nothing and a good look at the back
office.

The box is under the desk behind a false panel. Heavy. Combination
lock. You don't know what's in it. You know it's worth finding out.

It's nine-forty. The street is quiet. There are two ways in.

fields.title: Intro
fields.body:
You've been asking around. Carefully, through the right people,
in the right bars. Who moves stolen jewelry in this city without
asking questions. The same name comes back every time. Marek.
Operates out of a dry cleaner's on Ansel Street. Been there
fifteen years. Reputation for fair cuts and short memories.

What you're bringing to that meeting is your own business.

fields.title: Word On Marek
fields.body:
Marek's dry cleaner's has been dark for three days. Shutters
down, no sign, the kind of absence that means something. You've
been asking around — carefully, through the right people. Word
comes back that Marek hasn't skipped. He's still in the city,
staying low, waiting to see if the heat from the Ansel Street
job dies down. Someone puts a name to an address: a storage
facility off Crane Road he uses to stage goods before they move.
He's been seen there twice this week.

What nobody mentions — because nobody knows — is that Marek
has been asking around too.

fields.title: Summons
fields.body:
You didn't go looking for Marek. Marek found you. A note
under the door — no name, just an address and a time. You
know who it's from. You know what it means. Showing up is
already an admission. Not showing up would be worse.

The address is a bar on Fenwick Street called The Linden.
Marek is in the back booth, alone, drinking something clear.
He doesn't look like a man who's worried. He looks like a
man who's already decided how this conversation ends.

He tells you what you owe. Then he tells you how to pay it off.

fields.title: Four Days Out
fields.body:
Three years inside and the city didn't wait for you. The apartment
is exactly how you left it — which means it's a mess. Water stain
on the ceiling shaped like a boot. Radiator that clanks like it's
got opinions. The envelope of cash you came home with is already
half gone and your parole officer wants you in legitimate work by
the end of the month.

Your old crew hasn't called.

Then your phone buzzes. Your sister's name on the screen. You
haven't spoken since the trial. Three years of silence and she's
calling at nine on a Tuesday night. You stare at it for two rings.

fields.title: The Message
fields.body:
The message comes through a number you don't recognise. Three
sentences. Someone heard you handled a situation on Decker Avenue.
If you're looking for work, there's a collection job — six hundred
dollars owed to a mutual party. Meet at Dave's Bar, Flats, tomorrow
at noon. Come alone, obviously.

You don't know who Dave is. You know the bar. It's the kind of
place where the beer is cold and nobody remembers what you look
like when you leave.

Six hundred dollars. For a city that didn't wait for you, it's
paying attention now.

fields.title: Her Building
fields.body:
Decker Avenue looks the same as it always does. Except for the
grey sedan idling across the street. You clock the plates, clock
the two guys inside, clock the way they don't look at you when
you walk past. Professionals. Or close enough.

Your sister buzzes you in before you even knock. She's been
watching from the window.

She looks tired. Not scared — she's past scared. Just tired and
waiting to find out how bad it gets.

She hands you a card. Plain white. A name — Emilio — and an
address on the south side. It was slipped under her door this
morning. No note. Just the card.

fields.title: Inside
fields.body:
Watch cases and ring displays glinting in the light bleeding
through the blinds. You move through to the back office, find
the false panel, pull out the lockbox. Heavier than you expected.
Combination-locked — no time to crack it here. You tuck it under
your arm.

Then the alarm goes.

Not the one you expected. A silent sensor on the office door you
didn't clock. Somewhere across the city a monitoring board just
lit up. You've got maybe four minutes before a patrol car rolls.

fields.title: Marek
fields.body:
The dry cleaner's smells like chemicals and old coffee. Marek
is fiftyish, heavyset, reading a newspaper behind the counter
like he's been doing it since before you were born. He doesn't
look up when you come in.

You tell him what you've got. He sets the paper down. Looks
at you the way a man looks at something he's still deciding
the value of.

There's a man named Duvall, he says. Owes Marek four months
of interest on a loan he's been avoiding. Marek wants a
conversation had. Nothing permanent — just enough that Duvall
understands the situation has changed. Do that, come back with
confirmation, and Marek will take a look at the jewels.

fields.title: Crane Road
fields.body:
A row of orange roller doors under a sodium light. Unit 14
at the far end has a padlock that's been opened recently —
the ground around it scuffed, a coffee cup by the door. Inside:
empty crates, packing materials, a folding table with a city
map pinned to it. Marek's been planning something.

Then the roller door behind you comes down.

Three men. Marek's not among them. One of them says Marek
sends his regards.

fields.title: Negotiate
fields.body:
You contest the figure. Marek sets his glass down. The bar
is quiet enough that this is a real conversation, which means
it's also a real risk.

fields.title: Her Voice
fields.body:
She doesn't say hello. Just launches into it like she's been
rehearsing. There's a guy — Terrell — been coming around her
building. Says she owes him for something her neighbor ran up.
Wrong place, wrong association. Doesn't matter how it started.
What matters is he's been at her door twice this week and the
second time he put his hand on the frame and leaned in.

You ask if she's okay. Long pause.

She says she's fine. She just needs it to stop.

Another pause. Longer.

She didn't want to call you. She wants you to know that.

fields.title: Dave's Bar
fields.body:
Dave's Bar is half empty at noon, which is still more full than
you'd like. The contact is already there — booth in the back,
water on the table, hasn't touched it. He introduces himself as
Morris. Doesn't offer a hand.

He lays it out clean. Guy named Pruitt borrowed from Dutch's
operation eight months ago. Six hundred, short-term, standard
terms. Paid nothing. Dutch's regular guys are busy and Pruitt is
small enough that sending them is wasteful. Morris is offering
you a finder's rate — a hundred off the top if you bring back
the full amount. Fifty if you come back with a partial and a
credible story.

He slides a piece of paper across the table. Address in the Flats.
Apartment 4C.

He didn't want to call you. Dutch doesn't know your name yet.
This is how you get one.

fields.title: Emilio's Place
fields.body:
The address takes you to a dry cleaning business on Harrow Street.
Clean windows, closed sign, lights on in the back. The guy at the
door looks you over and steps aside without a word.

Emilio is sitting at a small table with two cups of coffee. He's
older than you expected. Neat. The kind of man who folds his
jacket before he sits down. He gestures to the chair across from
him like you're here for a job interview.

He lets the silence do the work for a moment. Then he says he
understands you had an interaction with one of his people.

fields.title: The Run
fields.body:
Through the alley, box under your arm, moving fast. A patrol car
sweeps its light across the far end. You press into a doorway.
Hold your breath. The light moves slow.

fields.title: Marek Pushback
fields.body:
You try to skip the errand, go straight to the deal. Marek
folds his newspaper in half and sets it on the counter. Waits.

fields.title: Stall
fields.body:
You buy time with words — calm, unhurried, like you're not
the one who's cornered. Like you walked in knowing exactly
what was waiting. The trick is making it convincing enough
that one of them blinks first.

fields.title: The Job
fields.body:
A man named Garrett runs a print shop on Ostler Street.
On the surface it's legitimate. Underneath, Garrett has
been producing forged documentation — IDs, titles, permits
— and selling them through a network Marek used to be part
of. Garrett cut Marek out six months ago and kept the network.

Marek wants two things: the plates Garrett uses for the
forgeries, and enough on Garrett personally to keep him
quiet afterward. Marek wants leverage, not violence.
For now.

fields.title: Why You
fields.body:
She lets the silence sit for a moment, like she's deciding how
honest to be.

Because there's nobody else. Because you're already the worst
thing that could happen to someone, so what's one more time.

It lands like she meant it to. You don't argue. There's nothing
to argue with.

fields.title: Pruitt's Address
fields.body:
The address is twelve minutes on foot. A residential block, older,
the kind where the buzzers haven't worked in years and everyone
just props the front door. Apartment 4C is on the top floor. The
building smells like cooking and old carpet.

You stand outside the door for a moment. Through it you can hear
a television. Some afternoon talk show. Pruitt's home.

Six hundred dollars. You don't know why he stopped paying. Could
be a lot of things.

fields.title: The Sit Down
fields.body:
Emilio lays it out simply. Terrell works for him. What happened
to Terrell was an insult, however justified the provocation.
Insults have costs. He's not angry about it — anger is
inefficient. He just needs the ledger balanced.

He slides a folded piece of paper across the table. A number.
You could pay it, if you had it. You don't. Which means you
need to give him something else — a reason to write it off.
You have about sixty seconds to figure out what that is.

fields.title: Cornered
fields.body:
Two officers. One car. They haven't drawn yet — the call came in
as a possible, not a confirmed. You still have the box. The street
is quiet enough that this could still go either way.

fields.title: Lockup
fields.body:
The 9th precinct evidence lockup is in the basement. You have
a contact — a civilian clerk named Priya who processes intake
and has been known to look the other way for the right amount
of cash. The box was brought in two nights ago. It's sitting
in a staging bin, not yet logged into the system.

Priya can get you in during a shift change. You're on your
own once the door closes.

fields.title: Brawl
fields.body:
Three men in a storage unit. The map on the folding table
is the only thing worth saving.

fields.title: Casing
fields.body:
Garrett runs the shop with one employee — a teenager who
leaves at five. Garrett stays until seven, alone. There's
a back entrance through the alley that doesn't get used
much. The forging operation is in the basement — you can
see the light through the pavement grate. There's a filing
cabinet in the back office visible through the window.
That's probably where the leverage is.

fields.title: Terrell
fields.body:
Your sister's building is a six-story walkup off Decker Avenue.
Terrell isn't hard to find — he's exactly where she said he'd be,
leaning against the stoop railing like he owns the block. Young.
Maybe twenty-five. Gold chain, clean sneakers, eyes that clock you
the second you round the corner.

He watches you approach without moving. You lost?

fields.title: Pruitt's Door
fields.body:
Pruitt opens the door on the chain first. Forty-something,
underdressed for the time of day, the specific look of someone
who has been waiting for this knock for eight months. He knows
exactly why you're here before you say anything.

He opens it the rest of the way. Doesn't invite you in. Just
steps back far enough that you're not in the hall.

The apartment is small and neat in the way that people who have
lost control of most things keep the things they can control very
tidy. A kid's drawing on the fridge. Soccer ball in the corner.

He says he doesn't have it. He says it like a man who has
rehearsed it and doesn't believe it will work.

fields.title: The Fight
fields.body:
The two guys from the back are efficient. This isn't personal for
them either. Just business. The table goes over. The coffee spills.
Emilio watches from his chair like a man waiting for a meeting
to finish.

fields.title: Talked Out
fields.body:
Wrong address, wrong night, wrong person. You spin it quietly,
without any particular urgency. The box is explained or hidden.
The trick is meaning it more than they doubt it.

fields.title: Lockup Grab
fields.body:
Inside the evidence room. Fluorescent hum, rows of shelving,
numbered bins. The box is where Priya said it would be — staging
bin seven, third shelf. Thirty seconds if nothing goes wrong.

fields.title: After The Brawl
fields.body:
You search the unit. The city map has three locations circled
in red marker — one of them is crossed out, which means two
still matter. There's also a phone on the folding table,
unlocked. Recent calls, one number that keeps coming up.

Sitting in the corner, zip-tied to a folding chair and furious
about it, is a woman. She was already here when Marek's men
arrived. She's been trying to get to Marek herself for two months.
Her name is Sable.

fields.title: The Shop
fields.body:
Inside the print shop. Ink smell and machinery. Award
certificates on the wall, a customer counter, a tip jar.
The legitimate front is convincing. The back room is where
it changes — a door with a keypad. You need to get through it.

fields.title: Squeeze
fields.body:
You tell him who you're there for. Then you tell him — quietly,
without any particular emotion — what happens if he comes back.
You don't raise your voice. You don't need to. The trick is
making sure he understands that you mean it more than he does.

fields.title: Lean On Pruitt
fields.body:
You don't touch him. You don't need to. You just take up space —
move around the apartment slowly, look at things, let the silence
do the work. The soccer ball. The drawing on the fridge. You pick
up a mug from the counter and turn it over in your hands.

Pruitt is sweating. He keeps looking at the door.

You ask him, very calmly, where the money is.

fields.title: Cracks
fields.body:
One of Marek's men — younger, less certain — starts filling
silence with information. Marek's moving tonight. The goods,
the cash, all of it. There's a woman they were supposed to
deal with too, already in the unit when they arrived. They
haven't decided what to do with her yet.

You use the distraction to move. Two uncertain seconds are
enough.

fields.title: Setup Deal
fields.body:
He lost his job seven months ago. The loan was meant to bridge a
gap that kept getting wider. He's got three hundred put back —
been scraping it together for weeks, planning to call and make an
offer, kept losing his nerve. The rest he doesn't have. He doesn't
say when he'll have it. You don't think he knows.

He's not running. He's not stupid enough to run with a kid in
school three blocks away. He's just a man who borrowed money and
had the wrong kind of year.

You could work with this. Morris said fifty for a partial and a
credible story. This is a credible story.

fields.title: The Deal
fields.body:
Emilio picks up his coffee. Takes a sip. Sets it down.

He says your sister's debt is cleared. The guys outside her
building will be gone by tonight. In return you owe him one
job — nothing violent, nothing that puts you back inside.
He'll be in touch when he needs it.

He says it the way you'd confirm a delivery time. No threat in
it. No warmth either. Just the transaction, concluded.

He stands. The meeting is over.

fields.title: Fought Out
fields.body:
It goes loud. They call it in before you're clear. The street
wakes up around you one light at a time.

fields.title: Lockup Cornered
fields.body:
A cop. Tired. End of shift. He hasn't raised the alarm yet —
he's still deciding what he's looking at. You still have a
way out of this if you move right.

fields.title: Sable
fields.body:
Sable ran errands for Marek eighteen months ago — courier work,
nothing heavy. Then Marek decided she knew too much about a
particular shipment and made her life difficult in the specific
way that people like Marek do. She's been building a picture
of his operation ever since.

She knows where he's going tonight. She also knows she can't
get there alone. She makes clear she prefers not to go there
alone.

fields.title: Keypad
fields.body:
Four digits. The business license on the wall has a year
on it. People are predictable when they think nobody's
watching.

fields.title: The Fight
fields.body:
It goes loud and fast. Terrell's got reach but no discipline.
The stoop light flickers. Somewhere above you a window slides shut.

fields.title: Deal Roll
fields.body:
You tell him the terms. Three now. Three by the end of the month.
You'll vouch for the arrangement. He needs to understand that your
vouching is the only thing standing between him and Dutch's actual
guys — who are less interested in payment plans.

He's listening. Doing the math. Trying to figure out whether
you're real or whether this is a different kind of pressure.

fields.title: Standoff
fields.body:
You're at an impasse. Pruitt's scared but he's not moving — either
because he genuinely can't or because he's decided he'd rather take
the consequence than produce money he doesn't have. Hard to say
which. Maybe he doesn't know either.

The kid's drawing on the fridge is of a house with a yellow sun.
The kind every kid draws. The sun has a face.

fields.title: Walked Out
fields.body:
Emilio doesn't run. He just stands there while you step over his
guys and walk toward the door. You'd almost respect it.

At the door he says your name. You turn. He says the debt still
stands. Fighting his people doesn't clear it — it adds to it.
He'll be in touch.

He says it without any particular feeling. That's the part that
stays with you on the walk back.

fields.title: Clean
fields.body:
Back at the apartment with the box on the table. You crack it
open twenty minutes later with a chisel and patience — the
combination lock gives at the hinge, not the dial. Inside:
a dozen pieces, mixed cut, wrapped in cloth. Rings, a bracelet,
something that might be a brooch. Nothing traceable on sight.

Nobody has your face. The city doesn't know your name.

You'll need to find someone who can move these. But that's
tomorrow's problem. Tonight you got out clean.

fields.title: Lockup Bluff
fields.body:
Wrong room, wrong floor. You say it like you mean it.
Like you're mildly inconvenienced, not running anything.

fields.title: Alone
fields.body:
You try to extract Marek's location without committing to
the alliance. Sable reads you immediately. She wants a
commitment, not a maybe.

fields.title: Garrett Confrontation
fields.body:
Garrett is fiftyish, ink-stained, the kind of man who's
spent decades being careful and knows when that's about
to stop mattering. He's not aggressive — he's calculating.
He looks at you the way people look at weather.

He asks who sent you.

fields.title: Backed Off
fields.body:
Terrell gets the message.

He pockets his phone slowly, looks at you one more time, and walks.
No scene, no last word. That's how you know he took it seriously —
the ones who aren't scared always need the exit line.

You text Vickie two words. It's done.

Three minutes later she replies. A single thumbs up. You stare at
it longer than you should.

fields.title: Full Collection
fields.body:
Six hundred in an envelope. He counted it out in front of you,
hands shaking slightly, like he was glad to be rid of it. You
don't know if that's true.

You fold the envelope into your jacket and go. Don't say anything
on the way out. There's nothing to say that would make it better.

Morris picks up on the second ring. You tell him it's done. He
tells you to come by Dave's tomorrow for your end.

fields.title: Roughed Up Win
fields.body:
Terrell's on the ground. His guys didn't stick around past the
first thirty seconds — loyalty has a pain threshold. You go through
his pockets before he changes his mind about staying down. A hundred
and fifty in crumpled bills. Pocket it.

Your ribs are going to say something about this tomorrow. That's
tomorrow's problem.

You text Vickie. It's done. No reply. The message sits on read.

fields.title: Partial Collection
fields.body:
Three hundred in a ziplock bag, warm from wherever he'd been
keeping it. He shakes your hand at the door. You let him.

Morris is less enthusiastic on the phone, but he accepts it. The
story is credible enough. You'll get your fifty when the rest
comes in — if it comes in. Morris doesn't say if. He doesn't
need to.

Dutch's operation got half its money and a payment plan from a
guy in a fourth-floor walkup. You tell yourself the other half
is coming. You're probably right.

fields.title: Walked Away
fields.body:
You leave Pruitt's building and stand on the pavement for a
moment. Nothing in your pocket. Nothing to show Morris.

You call it in anyway. Morris listens without interrupting. When
you finish he's quiet for a long time. Then he says Dutch will
be disappointed. Says it the way people say it when they mean
something worse than disappointed.

You tell him you'll find another way to make it right. Morris
says that would be a good idea.

fields.title: Done
fields.body:
Your sister calls that evening. The car is gone. She doesn't ask
what you said or what it cost you. You don't tell her.

She says thank you. First time in three years. It lands somewhere
quiet and stays there.

You've got a debt you didn't want and a man you don't know who
knows where to find you. Could be worse. Has been worse.

The corkboard has two jobs on it now.

fields.title: Beaten
fields.body:
You got out. Barely. The box is on the table and the jewels
are inside it, which is the part that matters. But someone
saw something — a partial, a face, a figure in an alley. The
city is a little warmer now.

You crack the box open. A dozen pieces, mixed cut. Worth
finding someone to move them. Worth being careful about how.

You're still in this. Just more exposed than you planned to be.

fields.title: Lockup Run
fields.body:
It goes loud. Not ideal in a police building. You move fast
and hope the layout is as simple as Priya said.

fields.title: Together
fields.body:
Marek is at a warehouse on the south docks, loading a van.
He's moving faster than expected — someone told him the
storage unit went wrong. You and Sable have a narrow window.

fields.title: Garrett Knows
fields.body:
Garrett sits down. Opens the back room himself. Pulls the
plates out from under the press and sets them on the
workbench. Then opens a drawer and puts a folder next to
them. He says Marek already knows what's in it — this isn't
new leverage, it's old leverage Marek lost access to when
Garrett walked.

Then he makes you an offer.

He'll give you everything Marek wants. But he'll also give
you something Marek doesn't know exists — a second folder.
The real one. Something that doesn't just keep Garrett quiet.
Something that would keep Marek quiet too. Permanently.

He wants you to decide whose leash you want to be on.

fields.title: Done Clean
fields.body:
Back in the apartment. Radiator clanking. The city outside doing
what it always does.

You didn't have to break anything. That's a version of tonight you
weren't sure you still had in you. The corkboard on the back of the
door has one job pinned to it. Could be worse starts.

Vickie sends a second message an hour later. Just: thank you. No
punctuation. She probably agonised over it for twenty minutes.

fields.title: Done Paid
fields.body:
A hundred dollars finder's fee and a name Dutch now knows.
That's not nothing.

Morris texted after. Two words: Good work. You've worked for worse
acknowledgements. The money goes in the drawer and you sit with
the quiet for a while.

The corkboard has a second job pinned to it now. You didn't put
it there. Morris must have slipped it under the door while you
were out. You're already on a list. Might as well be a useful one.

fields.title: Cut Off
fields.body:
One exit. If you can block it before he loads up, Marek
has nowhere to go. Sable's on the other side. Timing is
everything.

fields.title: Done Rough
fields.body:
Back in the apartment. Ribs complaining. The cash from Terrell's
pocket on the counter — a hundred and fifty. Not bad for a Tuesday,
considering.

Vickie never texted back. That's fine. You didn't do it for the reply.

The corkboard on the back of the door has one job pinned to it.
Could be worse starts.

fields.title: Done Partial
fields.body:
Three hundred and fifty in your pocket and a payment plan that
may or may not hold. Morris called it acceptable. That's the word he used. Acceptable.

You've been called worse.

The work is there if you want it. Dutch doesn't know your name
yet — not really. But Morris does, and Morris is the door.
That's how it starts with operations like this. Slow and useful
until you're just another piece of the thing.

fields.title: Marek Found
fields.body:
Same warehouse, same window. But you're alone and Marek
has one more man than you expected. You watch from outside
for a minute. There's a smarter angle here if you're patient
enough to find it.

fields.title: Wait
fields.body:
Patience. The warehouse has a rhythm — a loading pattern,
a blind spot on the north side where the van blocks the
sight line. If you move at the right moment, you're inside
before anyone knows it.

fields.title: Done Hard
fields.body:
Your sister calls that evening. The car is gone. You don't know
if Emilio pulled them as a gesture or because he's regrouping.
Either way she sounds like she can breathe again.

You don't tell her about the debt. Or that it got worse tonight
instead of better. She's had enough for one week.

You've got heat you didn't need and a man with a long memory who
now has a reason to use it. The city feels a little smaller than
it did this morning.

fields.title: Busted
fields.body:
Processed, printed, held overnight, released on a technicality
that won't hold twice. The box was confiscated before you found
out what was in it. Your parole officer is going to hear about
this. The job was a wash. Worse than a wash.

You walk out of the precinct into a grey morning with empty
pockets and a new entry on your record. The city looks exactly
the same as it did yesterday. It always does.

fields.title: Lockup Clean
fields.body:
Priya cracks the box in exchange for her cut — she has the
combination from the intake form. Inside: a dozen pieces,
mixed cut, wrapped in cloth. Rings, a bracelet, something
that might be a brooch. Nothing traceable on sight.

Same destination as always. Marek. Ansel Street.

fields.title: The Warehouse
fields.body:
Marek and two men. He's not a fighter but he doesn't need
to be — his men are. Sable takes one side. You take the other.

fields.title: Took Both
fields.body:
You reach for both folders. Garrett's eyes narrow. He wants
a commitment, not a maybe. He lets you know that.

fields.title: Beaten
fields.body:
You come to on the pavement outside your sister's building. Ribs
aching, wallet lighter. Someone took the liberty.

You don't go up. You can't face that conversation right now. You
just walk. The city swallows you up the way it always does —
indifferent, unhurried, completely unimpressed.

Your phone has no new messages.

fields.title: Done Burned
fields.body:
Nothing collected. Morris disappointed. Dutch a name attached
to a bad first impression.

You sit in the apartment and run through it. Pruitt's face.
The soccer ball. The drawing on the fridge with the smiling sun.
You're not sure you made the right call — you're not sure there
was one. That's the problem with jobs like this. The math never
works out clean.

The corkboard is empty. That might be temporary. It might not.

fields.title: Garrett Fight
fields.body:
Garrett is not a fighter but he has a back room full of
heavy equipment and the advantage of familiarity.

fields.title: Beaten
fields.body:
You come to in the alley behind Harrow Street. They were
professional about it — nothing broken, nothing that won't
heal. Just enough to make the point.

Your phone has a text from an unknown number. One line.

The debt still stands. We'll be in touch.

You don't text back. You just sit in the alley for a while
and let the city go about its business around you.

fields.title: Duvall
fields.body:
Duvall lives above a laundromat in a neighborhood that's given
up pretending. He answers the door in an undershirt, mid-forties,
the look of a man who's been waiting for something bad to happen
and is almost relieved it finally has.

You deliver the message. Physically. He doesn't fight back —
he just takes it, which is almost worse.

fields.title: Marek Cornered
fields.body:
Marek, cornered, does what cornered men do — he offers
something. Not the jewels, those are already moved. But
he has information. There's a buyer, a name, someone
further up the chain. He's offering it as a trade: walk
away, and the name is yours.

Sable, if she's here, doesn't trust it. The player has
to decide.

fields.title: Basement
fields.body:
The basement is exactly what the casing suggested. Plates,
chemicals, a professional setup that cost real money. You
take the plates. There's also a locked filing cabinet —
the leverage Marek wanted.

fields.title: Stay On Task
fields.body:
You take what Marek sent you for and leave Garrett's offer
on the table. Clean. Uncomplicated. You're still on Marek's
leash but you did the job right.

fields.title: Flip
fields.body:
Garrett says there's someone else who'd want to know what's
in this folder. A name: Sable. She's been building a case
against Marek for months. He gives you a way to reach her.
Says the debt between you is settled.

You walk out of the print shop with the folder and a name.
The plates stay on the workbench.

fields.title: Duvall Light
fields.body:
Duvall is more cooperative when the ask is cash rather than
pain. He counts it out slowly, hands it over, doesn't make
eye contact. Three hundred. You keep fifty off the top per
Marek's terms.

fields.title: Took The Deal
fields.body:
The name Marek gives is Callum Voss. Import business, clean
on paper, not clean underneath. Sable writes it down like
she already half-knew it. Marek walks. Neither of you watch
him go.

The jewels are gone. But Voss is a door, and doors are worth
more than jewelry.

fields.title: Sable Meeting
fields.body:
Sable is exactly what Garrett described — careful, thorough,
patient in a way that suggests she's been burned by impatience
before. She looks at the folder for a long time without saying
anything. Then she says it's enough. Not to take Marek apart
completely — but enough to make him move, and moving men
make mistakes.

She wants to work together. She has pieces. You have pieces.
Marek is the puzzle.

fields.title: Duvall Done
fields.body:
Back to Ansel Street. Marek nods when you walk in, folds his
newspaper, leads you to the back room. You put the jewels on
the table. He examines them one by one under a loupe, not
rushing. Names a number. It's fair — fairer than you expected.

He goes to the back to get the cash.

He takes a long time.

Then two men come through the side door instead.

fields.title: Took Everything
fields.body:
The name, the cash from the van, and Marek's phone. You
don't honor the deal. Sable doesn't object. Marek watches
you walk out with everything he has left and doesn't say
a word — which means he's already thinking about what
comes next.

So are you.

fields.title: Back To Marek
fields.body:
Back to The Linden. Marek examines the plates, examines
the folder if you brought it. Nods. Says the debt is cleared.
Says maybe there's more work if you want it. The way he says
it doesn't sound like an offer.

You're off the leash — for now. But you did Marek's dirty
work and you know it. And somewhere on Ostler Street, Garrett
is thinking about what comes next.

fields.title: Double Cross
fields.body:
The men aren't buyers. The jewelry is already in Marek's back
pocket. This was always how it was going to go. You have maybe
two seconds to change how it ends.

fields.title: No Deal
fields.body:
You didn't come here for information. Marek knows that now.
Sable zip-ties him to the van door — returning the favor —
and you're gone before anyone comes looking.

Clean. Uncomplicated. The kind of ending that doesn't
follow you home.

fields.title: Allied
fields.body:
The debt to Marek is gone — he just doesn't know it yet.
You and Sable have something better than cleared books.
You have a plan. She keeps the folder safe. You stay loose.
When the moment comes, you'll both know it.

Marek thinks he won. That's fine. Let him think it a
little longer.

fields.title: Fight Out
fields.body:
Two of Marek's men in a back room. The jewels are gone but
you might still get out.

fields.title: Worked Over
fields.body:
You come to outside — the storage unit, or the warehouse,
depending on how far you got. Sable is gone, or was never
there. Marek is gone. Whatever you came for is gone with him.

He's still out there, still moving, and now he knows you're
serious enough to be a problem. That's something. Not much.
But something.

fields.title: Independent
fields.body:
You took Sable's information and kept your own counsel.
She didn't argue — just said to remember her name when
it mattered. The debt to Marek is cleared, technically.
But you're holding something that could burn him, and
he has no idea.

That's a position. Not a comfortable one. But a position.

fields.title: Flipped
fields.body:
You move first. Marek's men aren't ready for that. It goes
fast and ugly and then it's quiet and you're walking out with
the jewels still in your pocket and Marek's cashbox under
your arm. Marek is zip-tied to his own chair with a face
full of regret.

The dry cleaner's will be empty by morning. Marek will be
a name people say carefully for a while.

He's made an enemy. So have you. The difference is you
saw it coming.

fields.title: Cleared
fields.body:
Debt cleared. Job done. You did what Marek asked and now
you're even — his word, for what that's worth. You walk
out of The Linden and the city swallows you up.

Garrett has the plates missing and leverage held over him.
Marek has what he wanted. You have your freedom back,
more or less.

It doesn't sit clean. But it's done.

fields.title: Escaped
fields.body:
Out the back door, empty-handed. The jewels are gone — Marek
has them, and by tomorrow so will whoever he sells them to.
But you got out, which is more than Marek planned for you.

He'll know your name now. That cuts both ways. You're a
problem he didn't finish, which makes you dangerous. It also
makes you a target.

You got out. That has to be enough for tonight.

fields.title: Garrett Won
fields.body:
Garrett put you on the floor and called someone to come
collect you. By the time you're back on the street, Marek
knows the job went wrong. The debt stands. He'll be in touch.

You walk back past the print shop on your way home. The
lights are off. Garrett's already gone.

fields.title: Taken
fields.body:
Marek's men walk you out the back and relieve you of
everything. The jewels, whatever cash you came in with,
the leverage you thought you had. Marek doesn't even come
out of the back room. He doesn't need to.

They let you go. Sending a message is cheaper than a body.
The message lands.

You walk back out onto Ansel Street with empty pockets
and a very clear understanding of where you stand.

fields.title: Busted Again
fields.body:
Second arrest in a week. The parole officer is definitely
going to hear about this one. The box is back in evidence.
Marek doesn't know your name.

That's the only thing that went right.

You sit in the holding cell and think about the particular
sequence of decisions that led here. It's a short list.
That's almost worse.
